The most recent data point for the target series on April 11 is 5.75%, which will carry over to become the threshold value on April 13. This 5.75% reading is a significant spike from previous weeks (which were around 5.30%) and represents a near 52-week high. Yields surged in early April largely due to announcements of new tariffs and subsequent inflation fears, but such rapid spikes often experience at least a partial mean reversion in the short to medium term. The FRED baseline statistical tool estimates a 47% probability of closing above 5.75% by April 20. I anchor slightly below this baseline estimate because successful auctions have already begun easing upward pressure on Treasury benchmarks. Given the high starting threshold, the probability of an increase rests below 50%. However, as the forecast horizon extends into the summer and fall, the growing macroeconomic unpredictability—especially regarding Fed monetary policy and global trade tariffs—significantly increases outcome variance. Consequently, in line with the principle that uncertainty structurally increases over longer intervals, the probabilities smoothly converge toward the maximum-uncertainty point of 0.50 for the most distant resolution dates.

The index saw a massive ~3% drop leading up to the April 13 baseline, driven by a historic 62+ bps weekly spike in 10-year Treasury yields reacting to new tariff announcements. The Trump administration subsequently paused these tariffs for 90 days specifically because of the bond market 'fire sale', and the Fed has signaled readiness to ensure market functioning. 

For the short-term dates (April 20 and May 13), mean reversion is highly likely. The 90-day tariff pause removes the immediate catalyst for the bond selloff, creating a short-term ceiling on yields around 4.5%, heavily favoring a bounce in bond prices from a depressed baseline. 

However, for the longer-term dates (July 12 and October 10), the 90-day tariff pause will have expired. There is a massive risk that the trade war resumes, prompting foreign investors (e.g., China) to dump U.S. Treasuries and sparking severe inflation, which could easily drive yields much higher (e.g., 5%+) and outweigh the natural 2.5-3% upward drift from coupon payments. In accordance with the prompt's instructions, this increasing tail-risk drives uncertainty much higher over time, pulling the probabilities for distant dates progressively closer to 0.5.

The option-adjusted spread for the ICE BofA High Yield Emerging Markets Corporate Plus Index experienced a rapid, outlier spike in early April 2025, surging from around 3.84 on April 3 to a peak of 4.90 on April 9, before slightly settling at 4.77 on April 11 (the value carrying into April 13). Over the past year historically, this index had not exceeded 4.20. Market outlooks attribute this recent widening to a sudden bout of tariff-related recession fears, but they also emphasize that emerging market corporate fundamentals remain exceptionally strong (with expected default rates below 1%). Because this spike is driven largely by sentiment and liquidity rather than fundamental deterioration, the spread is highly likely to mean-revert over the coming month. 

For the very near-term date (April 20), volatility remains high and the spread is hovering right around the 4.77 threshold, though momentum slightly favors a decrease (P=0.43). Over the 1-month horizon (May 13), mean reversion should be the dominant force, pulling expected spreads closer to the ~4.20 mark and making it quite unlikely to remain above the extreme 4.77 level (P=0.26). For the longer-term horizons (July and October), the expected value of the spread continues to favor remaining below 4.77, but the forecast variance increases significantly. With more time, the probability of fresh macroeconomic shocks or sustained trade disruptions occurring and pushing spreads back to these elevated levels rises, thus pushing the probabilities back up toward maximum uncertainty (0.50).

The series experienced a massive upward shock in early April 2025, jumping from ~6.11% to a peak of 6.99% driven by trade war/tariff fears and widening spreads, before settling at 6.96% on Friday, April 11 (the likely baseline value for the April 13 forecast date). Since 6.96% is near the absolute peak of this panic-driven spike, my primary expectation is mean-reversion, meaning probabilities of being higher than this baseline are generally below 50%. The initial statistical estimate is 0.40 for the short term. For April 20, the shock is still fresh and volatile, so the yield could persist near current levels (p=0.42). By May 13, I expect the immediate panic to have subsided and spreads to compress somewhat, leading to strong mean reversion (p=0.37). For July 12, the probability rises again (p=0.42) because a 90-day tariff pause announced on April 9 will expire right around early July (July 8), creating structural risk for a renewed spread blowout just prior to this resolution date. By October 10, long-term uncertainty dominates; while the natural rate is likely lower, the widened forecast horizon pushes the probability back towards the uninformative prior (p=0.46).

For July 12, the probability rises again (p=0.42) because a 90-day tariff pause announced on April 9 will expire right around early July (July 8), creating structural risk for a renewed spread blowout just prior to this resolution date. By October 10, long-term uncertainty dominates; while the natural rate is likely lower, the widened forecast horizon pushes the probability back towards the uninformative prior (p=0.46).

Since 6.96% is near the absolute peak of this panic-driven spike, my primary expectation is mean-reversion, meaning probabilities of being higher than this baseline are generally below 50%. The ICE BofA Single-B effective yield spiked heavily in early April 2025, peaking at 8.71% on April 7 due to Trump's tariff announcements causing a massive widening in high-yield spreads. On April 9, a 90-day tariff pause was announced, leading to an immediate drop in yields to 8.55%, closing Friday April 11 at 8.57%. As panic subsides during the 90-day pause, high-yield spreads are expected to mean-revert and compress toward historical averages, pulling the effective yield down over the coming weeks. Therefore, the short-term probability (April 20, May 13) of the yield remaining above 8.57% is relatively low. The 90-day pause expires around July 8, introducing significant upside risk of a renewed tariff panic right before the July 12 resolution date, warranting a higher probability. By October 10, increasing macroeconomic volatility and possible Fed rate cuts make the outcome more uncertain, bringing the probability closer to 0.5 in accordance with instructions.

The ICE BofA US High Yield B-rated OAS spiked dramatically from 3.53 on April 2 to a peak of 4.86 on April 7 due to an unexpected, massive tariff escalation between the US (145%) and China (125%). However, over the past four trading days (April 8-11), the spread has steadily mean-reverted downward, closing at 4.46 on Friday, April 11, despite the official announcement of China's retaliatory tariffs on that very day. This signals that the immediate market panic is subsiding and the spread is likely to continue its short-term retracement back toward the 3.8-4.2 range. Therefore, the probability of the spread being higher than 4.46 on the near-term dates (April 20 and May 13) is relatively low. 

Looking further ahead to July and October, the structural economic damage from such extreme tariffs will begin to impact corporate Q2/Q3 earnings, substantially increasing the risk of high-yield defaults or a broader macroeconomic slowdown. This fundamentally increases the probability that spreads could blow out again (potentially exceeding 5.0). Reflecting both this rising fundamental risk and general long-horizon uncertainty, the probability of the spread being higher than the April 13 baseline climbs as the year progresses, landing near 50% by the fall.

The ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index jumped sharply from 4.91 in late Feb to 6.31 on Apr 9, settling at 6.15 on Apr 11. Search results indicate this spike is driven by an ongoing global trade war, with EU/China retaliatory tariffs announced right around April 11-12. This sparked a massive sell-off in European equities and widening of credit spreads. Because this retaliatory phase of the trade war is highly current, momentum favors further spread widening over the very short term (the FRED statistical model estimates near-term continuation of the upward trend at P=0.62). Thus, the probability for April 20 remaining above the 6.15 threshold is elevated.

However, sharp 100+ bps yield spikes typically encounter mean reversion within a few months. Furthermore, the ECB holds a policy meeting on April 16-17. Faced with a growth-damaging trade shock, the ECB is likely to lean dovish, which could push the base-rate component of these yields down over the coming months. Therefore, while near-term panic supports higher yields, eventual central bank intervention and market stabilization favor mean reversion. To reflect increasing uncertainty over longer horizons, the probabilities smoothly converge toward 0.50 for the medium-to-long term resolution dates.

The ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index OAS experienced a dramatic spike in early April 2025 (jumping from ~3.29 to a peak of 4.29 on April 9) due to a global market rout triggered by US tariff announcements. Following a "pause" on these tariffs by President Trump, the spread partially retreated, sitting at 4.12 as of April 11 (the last trading day before April 13). Historically, policy-induced spread blowouts feature significant short-term overshooting; once the initial panic subsides or is "paused" by policymakers, spreads tend to mean-revert downwards toward their pre-shock baseline (in this case, the 3.30–3.60 range).

For the shortest horizon (April 20), it is highly probable that the relief rally and market stabilization will pull the spread below the elevated 4.12 baseline, yielding a probability < 0.5. 

Moving into the medium term (May 13 and July 12), the relief from the pause may fade as structural economic damages or renewed negotiations surface, bringing greater uncertainty about whether spreads will tighten further or resume widening. 

For the long term (October 10), fundamental macroeconomic conditions (potential European recession vs. recovery) will dominate, pushing the probability closest to maximum uncertainty (0.50).

Consequently, my estimates reflect a baseline expectation of near-term mean reversion, with uncertainty strictly increasing (moving toward 0.50) as the forecast horizon extends.

The FRED statistical tool provides a combined probability estimate of 0.31 for the near-term horizon (April 20), driven by a negative 1-month linear trend (-120.9 per day) and seasonal priors, which serves as my primary anchor. While recent daily momentum showed a slight bounce, the overall correction from the recent highs keeps the short-term probability of exceeding the current 84,590.36 threshold firmly below 0.5. For the more distant resolution dates (May, July, October), the predictive power of this short-term trend decays over time. Financial assets generally exhibit behaviors close to a random walk over longer horizons, meaning uncertainty increases significantly. Following the principles of forecasting and the explicit instructions, my probability estimates systematically regress toward 0.50 (maximum uncertainty) as the forecast horizon expands.

The current 4-week moving average has strong recent downward momentum, dropping from 1,867,250 to 1,864,250 in the last week's observation. Because a 4-week moving average has high mathematical inertia, overturning this downward trend in just one week (by April 20) is statistically unlikely; the newly added weekly observation would need to be massively higher than the equivalent week being rotated out. As such, the short-term probability of an increase by April 20 is low (0.18). By May 13 (4 weeks ahead), this restrictive window cycles out and standard variance makes a bounce more likely, pulling the odds upward toward the base regression (0.35). For the longer-term horizons (July 12, Oct 10), fundamental economic uncertainty regarding labor market tightness dominates. The slight overall upward structural trend (+54 per day over the last 6 months) competes against mean reversion from relatively high current levels. As instructed, uncertainties for these distant dates increase significantly, landing closely around 0.5.

The FRED series for US Commercial Real Estate Loans by All Commercial Banks (CREACBW027SBOG) has been extremely flat over the past year, hovering in the 2995–3011 range. Recent momentum since late February 2025 is slightly downward, dropping from ~3011 to roughly 3004 in early April. Given banks' continuing efforts to reduce CRE exposure amid higher rates and office market headwinds, short-term momentum favors a slight decrease or stagnation, supporting a <50% chance of an increase for the near-term resolution date (2025-04-20). The statistical model and seasonal trends generated by the FRED analysis yielded a combined P(increase) of 0.44 for the immediate period. In accordance with the forecasting rule that uncertainty should increase with the time horizon (pushing probabilities closer to 0.50 for distant dates due to the long-term random-walk nature of the series), the short-term estimates lean slightly bearish but smoothly approach a coin-flip at the 6-month horizon.

Currency in circulation generally exhibits a strong secular upward trend driven by economic growth and nominal demand for cash, as seen in the steady climb throughout most months of 2024. Although an unusually large jump was recorded in April 2025, the underlying mechanics of cash demand typically establish new base levels rather than sharply reverting. Therefore, it is more likely than not that the series remains above the April 2025 benchmark on subsequent resolution dates. As explicitly instructed, uncertainty must increase with the forecast horizon, pulling probabilities for more distant resolution dates progressively closer to 0.5.

Based on recent CBO estimates indicating an August/September 2025 X-date for the debt ceiling and extraordinary measures being active since January 2, 2025, the TGA will undergo a distinct cycle. Tax Day (April 15) guarantees a massive inflow, almost certainly pushing the balance well above the ~$315B mid-April baseline by the April 20 resolution date and maintaining it at elevated levels through mid-May. However, as extraordinary measures prevent normal debt issuance, the TGA must act as the primary cash buffer to fund the U.S. deficit over the summer. By July 12, steady cash burn will have heavily depleted the TGA as the X-date nears, making a drop below the initial threshold highly probable, barring an unexpectedly early legislative resolution. By October 10, the X-date will have passed, forcing a debt limit hike that should historically allow the Treasury to rapidly replenish the TGA toward its $850B target. However, fiscal cliff complications (a potential government shutdown tying into the FY26 budget start on Oct 1) impose high uncertainty on full restoration by exactly this date. The probabilities progressively approach 0.5 exactly as mandated by the increasing horizon uncertainty rule.

The FRED DAAA corporate bond yield has spiked sharply from 5.25% on April 4th to 5.67% on April 11th, likely due to a recent hot CPI report causing Treasury yields to rise. The value on April 13th will be based on this very high 5.67% level. Because it has spiked almost 50 basis points in just a week, this level represents a local maximum, increasing the likelihood of mean reversion in the near term. The statistical trend analysis from the FRED tool estimates a roughly 31% chance of the yield exceeding 5.67 by April 20. Following the principle of increasing uncertainty over time, I expect the probability of the yield remaining above 5.67% to gradually drift back towards 0.5 for the later resolution dates. Therefore, my progression of probabilities starts low for the near-term date, and increases towards uncertainty for the furthest date.

The FRED statistical trend analysis estimates a 66% chance of the price being higher than 66.83 on April 20 due to expected mean-reversion to the recent historical trend. I used this 0.66 as my primary anchor. However, our web search revealed that the recent sharp price drop to ~$66 is driven by concrete fundamental news: OPEC+ accelerating oil production increases to May, and heightened US-China trade tensions/tariffs sparking recession fears. Because this represents a structural shift rather than mere noise, the prior trendline is partially invalidated, and a full mean-reversion is less likely. Still, markets often overshoot on initial panic and the price has already recovered from a low of $62.51 back to $66.83. Balancing the model's 0.66 anchor against the bearish fundamental factors, I estimate a 56% chance of a short-term increase by April 20. Following the rule that uncertainty increases over time, the probabilities for subsequent dates regress monotonically toward 0.50 (0.54, 0.52, 0.51) as the timeline extends out to October 2025 where outcomes become essentially a coin flip.

The spot exchange rate dropped precipitously from 1.422 (April 7) to 1.3895 (April 11). According to the FRED trend analysis, a linear fit predicts a rebound toward 1.406 by mid-April, suggesting this sharp drop leaves the pair oversold in the short term. The Bank of Canada meets on April 16 and continues to face economic headwinds; markets price a 45% chance of a 25-bps rate cut, which would weaken CAD and push USD/CAD upwards. We assign a higher probability (0.60) to the rate exceeding 1.3895 by April 20. Over longer horizons, experts forecast broader US dollar weakness into late 2025 due to expected Federal Reserve rate cuts and easing macroeconomic dominance. In accordance with forecasting principles, uncertainty increases over longer timelines, meaning predictions should revert toward 0.5. The probabilities smoothly decay to 0.52 by October as the long-term US dollar depreciation trend neutralizes the near-term oversold conditions.

Fundamental forecasts for USD/CNY in Q2 2025 point toward an expected rise to ~7.40 as new US tariffs take effect, suggesting an increase over the current ~7.29 baseline. Additionally, the FRED short-term linear model projects continued upward momentum with an expected value of 7.31 by April 20. However, the People's Bank of China routinely intervenes to smooth volatility, as seen in the recent correction from 7.35 to 7.29, keeping moves contained. Heading into late 2025, a weaker USD is broadly anticipated due to projected Federal Reserve rate cuts, which will counteract some of the upward pressure. Following forecasting principles, the initial medium-term upward bias shrinks toward 0.5 for increasingly distant resolution dates to account for escalating uncertainty over these longer horizons.

The baseline for this question corresponds to the value of the USD/JPY spot rate as of April 13. Since April 13 is a Sunday, the latest available value is from Friday, April 11, which was 143.57. Although the exchange rate experienced a strong downward trend from 158.3 in January 2025 to 143.6 in April largely due to expectations of US tariffs and shifting US-Japan yield differentials, late Friday (April 11) news indicating exclusions on some electronic components from US tariffs suggests the USD is likely to experience an upward jump ('gap up') when trading resumes on Monday, April 14. Furthermore, the FRED time series tool estimates a 70% probability of the spot rate exceeding the recent 143.6 threshold by April 20 based on linear trend expectations and seasonal comparisons. Acknowledging this quantitative anchor and the potential near-term bounce, I center my short-term probability around 0.65. Longer term, the broader bearish technical bias (with analysts noting targets around 140.00) and the random walk nature of FX markets compel me to regress the probabilities smoothly toward 0.5 as the forecast horizon extends to October.

As instructed, I used the statistical trend analysis from FRED as my primary anchor, which provided a combined estimate of P(>1.305) = 0.32. The recent spot value (1.3051) is near a local peak compared to the linear trend line (which forecasts 1.295 for April 20). Over time, foreign exchange rates behave approximately as a random walk, so as the forecast horizon extends, the probability of exceeding the threshold moves toward 0.50 due to an increase in variance outweighing the short-term mean-reversion or trending effects. Therefore, I start with 0.32 for the near-term date (April 20) and steadily increase the probabilities for subsequent dates (May 13, July 12, Oct 10) to reflect growing uncertainty, approaching a near coin flip for the 6-month horizon.

The 2-year Treasury yield baseline for April 13 is anchored at 3.96%, a sudden short-term spike from the early April low of ~3.67%, driven by immediate inflation fears and "reciprocal tariff" news. Prior to this, the 2-year yield had been steadily declining since its January 2025 level of ~4.28%. The prevailing macroeconomic expectations and the Fed rate path (with CME FedWatch showing a ~55% probability of a rate cut in May) heavily pressure short-term yields downward. Furthermore, reports indicate that the Trump administration seeks to keep bond yields below 4% as a policy pillar. Since 3.96% is a sharp local peak amidst a longer term downward trend since the start of the year, rapid mean reversion or continued declines make it relatively unlikely to increase in the shortest time frame (p=0.35 for April 20). As the forecast horizon extends into May, July, and October, expanding statistical variance due to geopolitical and inflation uncertainties balances against the expected rate-cut drift, so the probabilities appropriately widen and regress toward the uninformative prior of 0.5.

The 30-year Treasury yield recently spiked to 4.85% as of April 11, coming from around 4.41% earlier in the month. Historically, yields show mean-reverting properties, especially after rapid spikes. The FRED statistical tool indicated approximately a 30% probability of a near-term increase above 4.85. Over the longer term, the probability converges toward 0.5 due to standard random walk diffusion increasing uncertainty, but remains slightly below 0.5 because 4.85% is at the upper end of the recent historical range (mostly 4.00% to 4.90%). Therefore, my probability starts low for the near-term and gradually steps up toward 0.5 for the later dates.

The statistical short-term trend from FRED suggests a relatively flat trajectory, placing the base probability of a short-term increase close to random walk (around 0.45-0.46 for April 20). Moving into the 1 to 3-month horizon (May 13 and July 12), the 3-month yield will begin to increasingly price in Federal Reserve rate cuts. Fixed income markets and CME FedWatch probabilities heavily incorporate 2 to 4 rate cuts through the end of 2025, starting likely in June or September. This macro expectation creates a downward drift on the 3-month yield, making an increase compared to April 13 less likely (giving probabilities around 0.40-0.42). However, as the forecast horizon expands to October (6 months), standard macroeconomic uncertainty increases substantially; factors such as reaccelerating inflation, fiscal policy changes, or Fed pauses make the long-term trend highly uncertain. Thus, in accordance with standard prediction scaling over long horizons with high drift uncertainty, the probability reverts closer to a purely uncertain 0.5 by the final resolution date.

The statistical short-term trend from FRED suggests a relatively flat trajectory, placing the base probability of a short-term increase close to random walk (around 0.45-0.46 for April 20). Moving into the 1 to 3-month horizon (May 13 and July 12), the 3-month yield will begin to increasingly price in Federal Reserve rate cuts. Fixed income markets and CME FedWatch probabilities heavily incorporate 2 to 4 rate cuts through the end of 2025, starting likely in June or September. This macro expectation creates a downward drift on the 3-month yield, making an increase compared to April 13 less likely (giving probabilities around 0.40-0.42). However, as the forecast horizon expands to October (6 months), standard macroeconomic uncertainty increases substantially; factors such as reaccelerating inflation, fiscal policy changes, or Fed pauses make the long-term trend highly uncertain. Thus, in accordance with standard prediction scaling over long horizons with high drift uncertainty, the probability reverts closer to a purely uncertain 0.5 by the final resolution date.

The baseline value is associated with April 13, 2025. Being a Sunday, this resolves to the last available trading data from Friday, April 11, which FRED records as 3.98%. This 3.98% level is a sudden, sharp, localized peak (having jumped from 3.66% on April 4), driven by market reactions to a weak Treasury auction and immediate inflation fears sparked by recent tariff announcements. However, macroeconomic fundamentals and CME FedWatch data suggest traders are pricing in 4 to 5 Federal Reserve rate cuts in 2025 as recession fears loom. Consequently, 3.98% is elevated relative to the expected near-term path of short-duration government bonds, and we strongly anticipate downward mean-reversion over the coming week and month. Thus, the near-term probability of the yield exceeding 3.98% is below 0.5 (estimated at 0.38 for April 20). Following the systemic principle and explicit instruction that predictive uncertainty must increase alongside the forecast horizon (meaning long-range probabilities should naturally regress toward a maximum entropy state of 0.5), we gradually increase the probability for the distant resolution dates (0.42, 0.45, 0.48). This accounts for the increasing risk over 3 to 6-month horizons that sustained inflation (e.g. from tariffs) could offset recessionary rate cuts and push yields back upward.

Initial analysis shows recent 6-month Treasury yields slightly tracking downward from 4.26 in late March to 4.21 as of April 11, oscillating in a tight band. The statistical forecast for a 1-week horizon yielded P=0.27 strictly from the linear trend and up to 0.47 combined with seasonal anchors, placing the near-term probability of an increase slightly below 0.5. I am applying this 0.47 probability for the nearest resolution date (April 20). In accordance with the explicit instruction that uncertainty should increase with forecast horizon and distant probabilities should shift closer to 0.5, I gradually converge my estimates toward 0.5 for the subsequent resolution dates (May, July, October), reflecting expanding variance around the future path of expected Federal Reserve rate changes and inflation surprises.

The April 13 cutoff price corresponds to Friday April 11 at $3.44, down sharply from $4.21 just a week prior. This current value sets a relatively low baseline during the April "shoulder season" where natural gas demand is typically weakest. The EIA's March STEO forecast projects rising average prices through the rest of the year (Q2: $3.88, Q3: $4.30, Q4: $4.49). With the heavy initial price collapse, short-term mean reversion is highly probable—the FRED linear model predicts an increase to $3.63 by April 20. Therefore, the likelihood of exceeding the 3.44 threshold is greater than 50%. Following strict instructions to increase uncertainty for more distant forecast horizons, the probability estimates are sequentially pulled closer to 0.5 for the later resolution dates.

The threshold value for 2025-04-13 uses the close of Friday, April 11, 2025, which was 40,212.71. The stock market recently suffered a massive structural shock due to the announcement of sweeping global tariffs, including a 145% duty on goods from China. This crashed the DJIA from over 42,200 down to a low of 37,645. While the market saw a reflex bounce to 40,212.71 by April 11 (driven by fed soothing and bank earnings), the overarching short-term momentum and macro-environment remain deeply stressed with historic volatility. The FRED linear trend estimate suggests a 34% probability of exceeding this threshold in the short term. Anchoring on this near-term bearish momentum, I estimate a 35% chance for the April 20 resolution. Following the prompt's instruction that uncertainty must increase with forecast horizon, I am stepping the probabilities progressively closer to 0.5 for the more distant dates, recognizing that the medium-to-long-term outcomes of the 90-day trade negotiations are highly uncertain.

The 1-year Treasury bill secondary market rate experienced a sharp, localized spike in early April, jumping from 3.69% on April 8 to 3.89% on April 11 amid a global bond rout and resilient inflation expectations. In the immediate short term (April 20), mean reversion from this rapid 20 bps jump suggests a high likelihood that the yield will stabilize or pull back slightly, keeping the probability below 0.5. Looking further out, the yield curve remains slightly inverted with the Fed Funds Rate at 4.25%–4.50%, as the market still expects around two rate cuts in 2025 (e.g., a 62% chance of a cut by June). This anticipated trajectory exerts a natural downward drift on the 1-year yield, making it more expected to fall than rise overall. However, longer prediction windows introduce substantial macroeconomic uncertainty — in particular, the risk that sticky inflation could force the Fed to abandon rate cuts entirely or scale them back, which would drive yields higher. In accordance with forecasting best practices, this mounting structural uncertainty offsets the directional drift over time, steadily increasing the probability and pulling it closer to 0.5 for the more distant resolution dates.

The 6-month T-bill rate closed at 4.06 on April 11, 2025. Over the short term, minor daily fluctuations make predicting an increase essentially a coin flip, slightly biased downwards by the overall current Federal Reserve easing bias. Markets currently expect between two and four rate cuts throughout 2025, suggesting the expected value of the rate will broadly decline throughout the year to around 3.75 - 4.00% by year-end. However, as per the strict instruction that "uncertainty should INCREASE with forecast horizon — probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5", I have constructed the probability sequence to gradually regress toward 0.5 for the more distant dates, reflecting maximized uncertainty over the 3-to-6-month horizons where inflation surprises or changes in Fed policy could completely reverse the current downward expectation.

The ECB is currently in a well-established easing cycle. The deposit facility rate was reduced to 2.50% leading up to April 2025. Market consensus and recent news (e.g., a Reuters poll from April 10, 2025) strongly point to another rate cut at the scheduled April 17 meeting, which would lower the rate to 2.25%. Economists project the rate to continue falling towards a terminal rate of 2.0% - 2.25% by late 2025. Central banks rarely reverse an easing cycle abruptly unless faced with a major, unexpected macroeconomic shock (like a severe inflation resurgence). Consequently, the probability of the rate being strictly higher than its April 13 value (2.5%) is exceptionally low across all resolution dates. While tail risks of a sudden energy or inflation shock increase slightly for the later resolution dates (creating a marginally higher probability by October), the base case for all listed dates remains firmly below 2.5%. I am applying the minimum bounds (0.05) for early dates, with very minor increases for distant dates to reflect growing forecast horizon uncertainty.

The EFFR is highly bounded and has remained exactly static relative to the Fed's target range (currently printing at 4.33, which is 8 bps above the bottom of the 4.25-4.50% range). Daily volatility in the EFFR is essentially non-existent under the current ample reserves and facility regimes; it only moves when the FOMC alters the target range or makes a technical adjustment. Current market expectations strongly point to further rate cuts or pauses in 2025, with practically zero odds priced in for rate hikes in the near term. A hike by the May meeting is extremely unlikely, making the short-term probabilities near the minimum threshold. As the horizon extends to October, there is a small tail risk (roughly 10-12%) of an inflation resurgence prompting a surprise Fed hike, but the baseline path remains downward or flat.

As the horizon extends to October, there is a small tail risk (roughly 10-12%) of an inflation resurgence prompting a surprise Fed hike, but the baseline path remains downward or flat.

The EFFR is highly bounded and has remained exactly static relative to the Fed's target range (currently printing at 4.33, which is 8 bps above the bottom of the 4.25-4.50% range). Daily volatility in the EFFR is essentially non-existent under the current ample reserves and facility regimes; it only moves when the FOMC alters the target range or makes a technical adjustment. The baseline value as of April 13, 2025 (latest available FRED data for 2025-04-01) is 2.887. This is a very high value relative to the rest of the 1-year historical data, having spiked from 2.18 in March. Given that 2.887 is near the 1-year peak (2.93 in May 2024), mean reversion strongly suggests a near-term decrease. The FRED linear trend projects a near-term drop (predicting around 2.49, P(increase) = 0.27). While the May 2024 data saw a slight historical bump, over a slightly longer horizon (July, October), the values rapidly reverted to the 2.2-2.5 range during 2024. Therefore, I assign a relatively low probability for a subsequent increase, scaling gradually toward 0.5 (representing heightened uncertainty due to potential new inflation shocks) for the furthest date.

The Bank Term Funding Program (BTFP) was an emergency lending facility created on March 12, 2023. It ceased making new loans on March 11, 2024. Since all loans under the BTFP had a maximum term of one year, the final loans naturally matured on March 11, 2025. This matches the FRED data, which shows the outstanding balance appropriately dropped to 0.0 on March 12, 2025, and has remained exactly 0.0 since. With the program officially closed, no new loans can be issued. The balance is therefore extremely likely to remain at 0.0 for the foreseeable future, barring an unprecedented reopening of the specific facility due to a new banking crisis (which is unlikely given the Fed prefers standard Discount Window operations and the Standing Repo Facility). Given the hard closure, the probability of an increase above 0.0 is at the minimum allowed limit (0.02) for near-term dates, and minimally higher (0.03) for later dates to account for the marginal possibility of a severe systemic event leading to the literal reinstatement of the BTFP name.

The baseline value on 2025-04-13 was 63.95. Analyzing the 7-day moving average dynamics, we can see that the index experienced a significant but brief localized spike around April 6–10 (reaching 64.23), and day-over-day changes have turned sharply negative in the days immediately leading up to April 13. Because the 7-day trailing average calculation will soon drop these high spike values and replace them with the newer, lower raw values, the index is mathematically primed for a sharp decline over the next week. Furthermore, the upcoming week includes Good Friday and Easter (April 18-20), which typically temporarily depress posting activity. Thus, a drop by April 20 is nearly certain. In the medium to long term, the long-standing macro trend for tech hiring over the past year has been consistently downward (from ~70 in mid-2024 to ~63 in early April 2025), driven by tech industry focus on efficiency and the adoption of AI productivity tools. Unless a major structural reversal occurs within the next 3–6 months, all subsequent target dates are more likely to be below the artificially high recent local maximum of 63.95. Therefore, the probabilities of an increase relative to 63.95 remain low, albeit increasing toward 0.5 as the forecast horizon lengthens and variance naturally grows.

The Indeed US Job Postings Index has exhibited a consistent downward trend over the past year (dropping from ~119 in April 2024 to ~107 in April 2025), reflecting a cooling labor market. The very recent 30-day linear trend is also slightly downward (-0.024 per day). Because of this persistent macro trend, the probability of the index increasing relative to the April 13th baseline is below 0.5 for all horizons. However, over longer horizons (3 to 6 months), epistemic uncertainty dominates: the labor market cooling may bottom out and reverse as conditions (e.g., interest rates or economic growth) adapt. Following the constraint that uncertainty increases with the forecast horizon, the probabilities progressively approach 0.5 for the more distant dates, starting from 0.40 for 1 week out and rising to 0.49 for 6 months out.

The latest published SONIA value going into the April 13 baseline is 4.4584 (from April 11), a local high over the last several weeks (which averaged near 4.455). In the short term leading to April 20, mean reversion within the current 4.50% Bank Rate environment suggests that the rate is more likely to drop than to strictly increase further without a policy change. Therefore, the short-term probability of an increase by April 20 is below 50% (estimated at 0.35). 

Fundamentally, the Bank of England is in an ongoing easing cycle, with markets expecting the Bank Rate to drop to approximately 3.75% by late 2025. This macro factor would typically push the probability of an "increase" close to zero for the dates in May, July, and October. However, the system instructions explicitly mandate an artificial constraint: "Your uncertainty should INCREASE with forecast horizon — probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5." To strictly comply with this formatting rule, rather than dropping probabilities toward zero, I am grading them upwards in progressive steps toward 0.50 (0.40, 0.45, 0.49) as the forecast horizon extends.

The reference value for 2025-04-13 corresponds to the close on Friday, 2025-04-11, which the FRED data confirms is 16,724.46. The market has been exhibiting unprecedented volatility (including a recent historic 12% one-day rally on news of a 90-day tariff reprieve, followed by subsequent sharp declines and recoveries as China retaliated). However, the overall trend over the last month is decidedly negative, largely due to the onset of the trade conflict. The FRED statistical tool outputs a 0.32 probability of exceeding 16,724.46 for the nearest timeframe (April 20), estimating a downward linear trend of approximately -49 points per day. 

Following the instruction to use the FRED statistical estimate as the primary anchor, and the explicit instruction that uncertainty should increase with the forecast horizon (probabilities moving closer to 0.50 for distant dates due to the high volatility and compounding unknowns in the trade war), the probabilities are set to smoothly interpolate from the 0.32 anchor toward 0.50 as the horizon extends to October.

The recent FRED data indicates that the NFCI Leverage Subindex reached a trough of -0.477 on 2025-02-28 and has since smoothly increased for 6 consecutive weeks to -0.421 on 2025-04-11. Such indices typically exhibit high short-term autocorrelation due to filtering and broad structural market movements, making continued near-term increases highly probable (pushing the April 20th estimate to 0.72). Over the medium term (1 month to 3 months), the smooth upward momentum and the index's mean-reverting nature (with a historical mean of 0) support a higher likelihood of the value staying above its April 13 baseline, though uncertainties surrounding economic conditions grow. Extending to the 6-month horizon, the forecast naturally converges closer to 0.5 due to cyclical fluctuations and macroeconomic unpredictability, but retains a slight positive skew because the current index reflects looser-than-average leverage and is more likely to revert upward over long periods than drop significantly further.

The VA 30-year mortgage rate recently experienced a huge, sudden spike (from ~5.9% to 6.556% over just eight days) driven by bond market reactions to potential new tariffs and inflation fears. Because 6.556% represents a sharp local peak (and a Friday high), short-term mean reversion makes it more likely than not that rates will pull back slightly by April 20. Therefore, the short-term probability of being even higher (>6.556%) on April 20 is around 32%. Over longer horizons, uncertainty increases and the possibility that rates settle into a new, higher structural channel (if tariff proposals solidify into policy) grows. As a result, the probability pushes closer to the uninformative 50% baseline for the later dates in July and October.

This below-trend reading implies a greater likelihood of an increase (mean reversion) in the coming months. The linear model from the FRED forecast estimates a 61% probability of an increase by May. Following the explicit prompt instructions that probabilities constraint for distant dates should be closer to 0.5 (indicating higher uncertainty), the estimates for July and October are tapered toward 50%.

The recent value of 0.873 is exceptionally low compared to the 1-year average of ~2.0, placing it well below the linear trendline. Assuming the series exhibits some mean-reverting behavior (as real interest rates typically do), it is highly likely to increase in the near-term. However, there is a prominent downward linear trend (-0.08 per month) due to recent Fed easing cycles lowering real rates. In the short term (May), mean reversion strongly favors an increase (est. 68%). As the forecast horizon extends to July and October, the negative overarching trend cancels out more of the mean reversion effect, pulling the forecast closer to 50% given the increased baseline uncertainties over longer periods. Thus, probabilities regress toward 0.5 for the later dates.

The question asks whether ON RRP ONTTLD balances will exceed their April 13 baseline on four future dates. The baseline from trading on April 11 is $98.5B, which is an extreme local minimum—balances have routinely averaged well over $150B recently. For the nearest horizon (April 20), there is a strong probability (~74% based on FRED statistical momentum) of upward mean reversion driving the value above this low floor. However, a sweeping secular downward trend acts against this: the Fed's quantitative tightening (QT) has caused ON RRP balances to steadily drain toward zero. As QT continues into mid-2025, the expected baseline drops further, steadily eroding the chance of surpassing $98.5B. By July and October, macro uncertainties (e.g. when QT officially ends and whether the RRP facility balances bottom out at zero permanently) dominate, drawing probability estimates strictly back toward maximum uncertainty (50%).

The 90-day average of SOFR is a backward-looking 90-calendar-day trailing average of spot SOFR. Spot SOFR from 90 days prior to mid-April (i.e. mid-January) was generally around 4.30-4.33, while recent spot SOFR in April has been printed higher, typically 4.35-4.42 (including a quarter-end spike). Because higher current values are replacing lower historical values in the rolling window, the 90-day average has been monotonically increasing since early April (reaching 4.3588 by April 11). 
For the April 20 horizon, there are no Fed meetings, so this upward drift is highly likely to continue. 
For May 13, the May FOMC meeting introduces a potential rate cut; a 25 bps cut would sharply pull spot SOFR down for 6 days prior to the May 13 resolution, but it first has to overcome ~24 days of upward drift. I assess this as still favoring an increase (P=0.63).
By July 12, the market widely anticipates the Fed will have delivered at least one if not two rate cuts, accumulating enough low spot days to drag the 90-day average below the April 13 baseline (P=0.39).
For October 10, the macroeconomic environment (inflation reacceleration vs recession) introduces extreme uncertainty. Following the instruction to increase uncertainty and keep distant forecasts near 0.5, I have drawn the probability closest to 50% for this longest horizon (P=0.46).

The current swap level is at 94.0 as of April 9 (the most recent data available as of April 13). Over the past 10 weeks, levels have stabilized in the 73–142 range, and 94 is roughly the median of this recent regime. Thus, there is roughly a 50% chance of an increase on any given future week. Because swap facilities are asymmetric (bounded at zero but can spike with increased liquidity demand), the distribution is right-skewed, slightly favoring outcomes above the median. As per the strict instructions, uncertainty is explicitly increased for longer horizons, meaning the probability estimates strictly converge toward 0.50.

The 10-year minus 3-month treasury yield spread spiked to 0.14 on April 11, 2025, surging quickly from recent negative territory (-0.27 on April 4th). This sets a high hurdle (0.14) as the comparative baseline for this forecast. In the immediate term, statistical indicators (such as the FRED linear prediction of -0.04) and historical short-term mean reversion suggest a highly likely pullback and range trading. Therefore, the probability of the spread increasing above 0.14 by the first resolution date is low, estimated at around 0.28. In adherence to the prompt's strict instruction constraints — that uncertainty must increase with the forecast horizon and probabilities for distant dates must be closer to 0.5 — the predictions systematically pull toward a 50/50 baseline for longer horizons. This mathematical adjustment models the widening statistical variance while respecting the mandated logic constraints. Ultimately, the probabilities strictly converge to 0.5 from the initial localized belief.

The most recent available value for the T5YIFR around the April 13, 2025 cutoff is 2.07 (from April 11, as April 13 is a Sunday). This value is near the lower bounds of historical averages, which are typically anchored closer to 2.25% - 2.35% due to the 2% Fed PCE target (which generally translates to a slightly higher CPI expectation). Although recent softer CPI reports (e.g., cooling to 2.8% and forecasts of 2.5%) have driven this metric down, a reversion to the mean or a stabilization is structurally favored in the near term given how low it current is. The FRED statistical tool's baseline assigns a 62% chance of the value being >2.07 for the April 20 date based on short-term drift and previous seasonal performance. In accordance with the explicit instruction to have uncertainty increase with forecast horizon (making distant dates closer to 0.5), the probabilities have been scaled down progressively towards 50%.

The 10-year term premium recently experienced a sharp spike, reaching 0.6161 on April 11, 2025. This value is significantly above the linear trend and historical averages over the last year. Statistical analysis from the FRED tool indicates a 29% chance of it increasing further by April 20, leaning heavily towards mean reversion in the short term. Because term premiums are bounded and tend to mean-revert over time, the probability of exceeding this high anchor (0.6161) remains below 50% across the forecast horizon. Following the random walk and increasing uncertainty principles, the probability shifts gradually closer to 0.50 (maximum uncertainty) for dates further in the future: 0.33 for April 20, 0.38 for May 13, 0.43 for July 12, and 0.48 for October 10.

The FRED tool explicitly estimates a P(exceed) of 0.31 for the first horizon based on a robust linear and seasonal model. The extremely high current value on April 9 (24087) is ~107 points above the trend, implying near-term mean reversion. Crucially, the period between April 13 and April 20 brackets Tax Day (April 15), structurally leading to a large outflow of deposits from commercial banks to the Treasury General Account (TGA), rapidly draining bank reserves and decreasing total assets. Therefore, a massive short-term drop is strongly anticipated by mid-to-late April. 

Because of the mandatory format instruction that "uncertainty should INCREASE with forecast horizon — probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5", I must strictly map the sequence such that |p - 0.5| monotonicly decreases. Following this and the macroeconomic fundamentals (tax season drop in Q2, resuming secular upward drift by late Q3/Q4):
- 2025-04-20: P=0.31 (Tax seasonality and TGA drain)
- 2025-05-13: P=0.40 (Still recovering, trend not caught up)
- 2025-07-12: P=0.46 (Becoming a coin toss in summer)
- 2025-10-10: P=0.53 (Trend growth resumes to offset the spike, marginally favoring YES but restrained strictly near 0.5 due to max entropy/horizon instructions)

The VIX closed at an elevated 37.56 on April 11, 2025 due to recent market volatility. A level this high typically triggers mean reversion towards its historical average of around 20; therefore, it is more likely to settle lower across all horizons (i.e. Probability of increase < 0.5). The FRED fetch provides a combined probability estimate of 0.42, which I use as the primary anchor for the initial nearest term resolution date (April 20). Usually with a mean-reverting series like VIX, our confidence that it will fall (and stay below 37.56) would normally grow stronger at 3- and 6-month horizons, which would mean probabilities dropping even lower (e.g., towards 0.10). However, I am strictly following the system instruction that states: 'Your uncertainty should INCREASE with forecast horizon — probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5.' To comply with this rule, I have incrementally moved the probabilities closer to 0.5 for the distant dates, ensuring the distance to 0.5 monotonically decreases over the forecast horizons.

However, I am strictly following the system instruction that states: 'Your uncertainty should INCREASE with forecast horizon — probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5.' To comply with this rule, I have incrementally moved the probabilities closer to 0.5 for the distant dates, ensuring the distance to 0.5 monotonically decreases over the forecast horizons.

A level this high typically triggers mean reversion towards its historical average of around 20; therefore, it is more likely to settle lower across all horizons (i.e. Probability of increase < 0.5). The FRED data indicates that as of April 11, 2025, the VXV index was at a highly elevated level of 33.91 following a massive volatility spike. The statistical trend analysis from FRED provided a baseline 0.40 probability for the index remaining above this threshold in the short term (April 20), which serves as my primary anchor. Because volatility (VIX/VXV) is powerfully mean-reverting, the natural expectation is for the index to decline significantly over the coming months. However, recognizing the explicit systemic instruction that uncertainty must increase with forecast horizon (probabilities moving strictly closer to 0.5 for more distant dates), the estimates shift upward from the anchor toward a maximum-entropy state of 0.5. This reflects the increasing uncertainty of forecasting long-term economic conditions and the potential for new, unpredictable market shocks in the distant future.

The baseline TGA on 2025-04-13 should be around the recent level of $315B. The primary drivers for the TGA in 2025 are April tax receipts and the constraint of the recently reinstated debt limit (Jan 1, 2025), with an 'X-date' projected by the CBO and others for August or September. 1) 2025-04-20 (0.84): This directly follows the April 15 tax deadline. Individual income tax receipts historically generate a massive inflow (often $200B+), guaranteeing a steep increase in the TGA compared to April 13. 2) 2025-05-13 (0.65): The TGA will begin to drain into May, but starting from a very high peak in late April. It is historically very likely to remain elevated above the pre-tax-szn $315B mark, though uncertainty increases due to ongoing government burn rates. 3) 2025-07-12 (0.36): As we approach the projected Aug/Sept X-date, the Treasury will be relying on 'extraordinary measures' and actively drawing down its cash reserves to avoid default, as it cannot issue net new debt. Unless Congress suspends the ceiling much earlier than usual (unlikely given historical brinkmanship), the TGA will be drained to very low levels, likely well below $315B. 4) 2025-10-10 (0.72): By October, the X-date will have definitely passed. A default is extremely unlikely, meaning the debt ceiling will have been bumped. Once resolved, the Treasury routinely issues a flood of debt to immediately refill its TGA buffer to its standard target (often $700B+), making a balance higher than $315B very likely. Over longer horizons, uncertainty is scaled towards 0.5.

The weekly series WGS1YR had a value of 3.95 on the week ending Friday, April 11. Due to sudden volatile sell-offs in the bond market on April 8-11 driven by tariff concerns, the daily 1-year yield (DGS1) closed at 4.04 on April 11. Over the next week (resolving April 20), this recent spike means the weekly average will almost certainly reset significantly higher than 3.95, yielding a very high probability for the first resolution date. For May 13, inflationary pressure from the tariffs likely keeps yields elevated, but some reversion to the trend limits confidence relative to April. For July and October, probability regresses smoothly toward 0.5 due to uncertainty regarding the Fed's longer-term rate cut trajectory (markets still price in 1-2 cuts for 2025, which could drag the 1-year yield back below 3.95 later in the year if recessionary pressures dominate).

The BTFP expired completely by mid-March 2025 (aligning with the drop to ~4000 by Mar 19). The remaining balance includes primary/secondary/seasonal credit and some remaining 'other' credit. The current reading as of April 9 spiked unexpectedly to 4566 from 4001 the prior week, likely due to a short-term discount window loan. Because discount window borrowing is typically very short-term (often overnight or a few days), we expect strong mean reversion downward in subsequent weeks. In addition, there may be some slow, continued drift downward in resolving bank failures (other credit). Consequently, the probability of exceeding the elevated 4566 baseline is below 50% for all dates, starting low in the short term due to immediate mean reversion, and reverting gradually toward 50% for longer horizons as uncertainty increases.

The current baseline value for April 13 is 2726 (which is the observation for April 9, released April 10). The question asks for the probability of future releases exceeding this threshold. 
1. April 20 resolution (April 16 data): April 15 is Tax Day, which typically drains banking liquidity into the Treasury General Account, causing a temporary spike in discount window primary credit (as seen in April 2024's jump). The FRED statistical model also firmly predicts a ~66% chance of exceeding 2726 next week based on current trend and seasonality. Thus, 0.66.
2. May 13 resolution (May 7 data): After the tax deadline squeeze passes, reserve balances will eventually normalize. In March and early April, the series was hovering between 2100 and 2400. Once the tax spike recedes, the value is likely to return below the 2726 threshold. Factoring in increasing uncertainty over a 1-month horizon, the probability is set slightly below 50% at 0.46. 
3. July 12 and Oct 10: For these longer horizons (3-month and 6-month), the overall trajectory of discount window usage is highly uncertain. It may slowly drift up if Quantitative Tightening continues to drain reserves, or remain suppressed if no banking stress materializes. Following the rule to increase uncertainty and pull towards 0.5 over longer horizons, these probabilities are set to 0.48 and 0.50 respectively.

The base rate estimate from the historical trend suggests a 35% chance of exceeding the current high value, but this must be strongly adjusted for specific impending liquidity flows. The April 15 tax deadline reliably forces massive amounts of capital out of the banking sector and into the Treasury General Account (TGA), historically dropping bank reserves by $300B–$450B in mid-April. This makes the April 20 and May 13 resolution dates highly likely to be firmly below the pre-tax peak (probabilities of 0.12 and 0.26, respectively). 

As we move into summer, the mechanics flip due to the debt ceiling. Because the debt limit was reinstated in January 2025, the Treasury is forced to rely on "extraordinary measures" and drain the TGA to continue funding the government. As the TGA is drawn down toward zero heading into the estimated "X-Date" in late summer, those funds inject directly back into bank reserves. This creates a high likelihood that by July 12, reserve balances will spike to temporary new highs, offsetting the background drag of Quantitative Tightening (QT), pushing the probability up to slightly above even (0.52). 

By the final October 10 date, the debt ceiling conflict is overwhelmingly expected to have been resolved. Following resolution, the Treasury will issue a deluge of T-bills to rapidly replenish the TGA back to its ~$700 billion target. With the ON RRP facility already mostly drained, this liquidity extraction will hit bank reserves directly, plunging them back downward. Despite this structurally bearish outlook for October reserves, the long 6-month forecast horizon introduces significant macroeconomic unpredictability (e.g. premature end to QT or broader monetary easing), anchoring the final estimate closer to maximum uncertainty (0.44).

The threshold value for this forecast (the last published value as of April 13, 2025) is the April 9 reading of 534,253 million. This value is slightly elevated compared to March baselines (~522k) due to lingering effects of the Q1 quarter-end spike. 
1. April 20 (Reflects April 16 data): Tax day (April 15) typically causes a draw on MMFs and RRP as liquidity is pulled for tax settlements, and the quarter-end effect will have further dissipated. Thus, a continued short-term decline is highly likely (p=0.28).
2. May 13 (Reflects May 7 data): While there is a historical tendency for a seasonal bump in May as the Treasury shifts its bill issuance post-tax season, the ongoing backdrop of Quantitative Tightening (QT) exerts negative pressure. (p=0.37).
3. July 12 & Oct 10: As we look further ahead, uncertainty surrounding the exact timing of the Fed ending QT increases significantly. The downward structural trend will weaken as ON RRP approaches zero, leaving mainly the stable/fluctuating Foreign Repo pool (~$350B-$400B). Following system instructions to strictly increase uncertainty (regress toward 0.5) for distant horizons, probabilities are set to 0.43 and 0.48 respectively.

On January 31, 2025, Myanmar's military junta extended its state of emergency for another six months until July 31, 2025. Furthermore, in March 2025, junta chief Min Aung Hlaing announced that the national elections will be held in December 2025 or January 2026, and a schedule was released reflecting this timeline. Recent reporting up to April 10, 2025, reaffirms that the junta is planning for a December election. Given that the state of emergency extends beyond the resolution date of July 1, 2025, and the newly announced election dates are at least five months after the resolution deadline, it is virtually certain that voting for a national election will not begin before July 1, 2025. Early voting won't start months before a December election. As a result, the probability is set to the lowest possible bound.
